title: The Shadow on the Wall: How One Operation Connects Everything
subtitle: Dot products, Fourier transforms, cosine similarity, and complex rotations are all the same thing. Here's why, with right triangles.

children.children: Forget math for a moment. Imagine you're standing outside on a sunny day, holding a stick at an angle. The stick casts a shadow on the ground.

Now rotate the stick. When it's horizontal (parallel to the ground), the shadow is as long as the stick. When it's vertical (pointing straight up), the shadow vanishes. At any angle in between, the shadow has some intermediate length.
That shadow is the most important concept in all of applied mathematics. It answers the question: how much of THIS points in the direction of THAT?
Everything in this tutorial — dot products, Fourier transforms, cosine similarity, complex rotations — is about computing shadows. If you understand the shadow, you understand all of it.

props.title: The Right Triangle: Where Cosine Comes From

children.children: The ancient Greeks studied right triangles obsessively. They discovered something remarkable about the ratios of the sides.
Setting Up the Triangle
Draw a right triangle. Label the angle at the bottom-left as θ (theta). The three sides are:

/|
               / |
              /  |
  hypotenuse /   | opposite
            /    | (vertical)
           /     |
          / θ    |
         /-------+
         adjacent
        (horizontal)

items.definition: The longest side (opposite the right angle). The 'stick' itself.
The side next to angle θ, along the ground. The 'shadow'.
The side across from angle θ, going up. The 'height' above the ground.

children.children: The Key Ratio: Cosine
Cosine is defined as:
cos(θ) = adjacent / hypotenuse = shadow length / stick length
This tells you: what FRACTION of the stick's length shows up in the shadow?
Let's Use Real Numbers
Suppose the stick (hypotenuse) is 10 units long.

rows: Stick lies flat. Shadow = full length. TOTAL alignment.
Slightly tilted. Most of the stick still projects horizontally.
Equal parts horizontal and vertical. Shadow is 71% of stick.
Mostly tilted up. Only half projects onto the ground.
Stick points straight up. NO shadow at all. ZERO alignment.
Stick points the OTHER way. Shadow is reversed. ANTI-aligned.

children.children: This table IS the entire story. Cosine measures 'how much of this thing lies along that thing.' 1 = total overlap, 0 = nothing in common, -1 = perfectly opposite. Every application we'll see is just this table in different costumes.
A vector is just an arrow — a direction and a length. In 2D, it's two numbers: how far right (x) and how far up (y).
Two Vectors, One Question
Let's say we have two vectors:

Vector a = [4, 3]  → points right 4, up 3
Vector b = [1, 2]  → points right 1, up 2

          ↑ y
          |
        3 +        a = [4,3]
          |       /
        2 +   b  /
          |  / \ /
        1 + /  θ
          |/
     -----+--------→ x
          0  1  2  3  4
Question: How similar are these vectors? How much does b point in the same direction as a?
This is the shadow question! Drop a perpendicular from b onto a. The length of that shadow tells you how much of b 'agrees with' a.
Computing the Shadow: The Dot Product
The dot product is the formula for computing this shadow (times the length of a):
Multiply corresponding components, then sum. That's it. But WHY does multiplying-and-summing compute a shadow?
Why Multiply-and-Sum = Shadow
Think about what each multiplication does:
(4)(1) = 4: Both vectors point right. They AGREE in the x-direction. Positive contribution.
(3)(2) = 6: Both vectors point up. They AGREE in the y-direction. Positive contribution.

If b pointed LEFT (negative x), the first term would be negative — they'd DISAGREE in that dimension. The sum aggregates all the evidence: dimensions where they agree (positive) and disagree (negative). The total is the net alignment.

a = [4, 3]
c = [-2, 1]

a · c = (4)(-2) + (3)(1)
      =   -8    +   3
      =   -5

Negative! c mostly points AWAY from a.
The Full Formula
The dot product connects to cosine through:
a · b = |a| × |b| × cos(θ)
Where |a| is the length of a, |b| is the length of b, and θ is the angle between them. Let's verify with our numbers:

When people say 'cosine similarity' in machine learning, this is ALL they mean: compute the dot product, divide by both lengths, and you get a number between -1 and 1 that measures how much two vectors point the same way. It's the shadow ratio.

props.title: The Leap: From 2D Arrows to Signals

children.children: Now here's where it gets interesting. A vector doesn't have to be 2D. It can have as many dimensions as you want.
Consider a signal — say, a sound wave sampled at 8 points in time:

signal = [0.7, 0.9, 0.3, -0.4, -0.8, -0.6, 0.1, 0.5]

This IS a vector in 8-dimensional space.
Each time point is a dimension.
The value at that time is the coordinate.
And a sine wave at some frequency, sampled at the same 8 points:
The Fourier question — 'how much of this frequency is in the signal?' — is LITERALLY a dot product:

The Fourier transform is a dot product between your signal (an 8D vector) and a sine wave (another 8D vector). It computes the shadow of your signal onto that sine wave. A big shadow means the frequency is strongly present. No shadow means it's absent.
Why Multiply-and-Sum Detects Frequencies
The same intuition as before: multiplication rewards agreement, punishes disagreement.
When the signal is positive AND the probe sine wave is positive → positive product → evidence FOR this frequency
When the signal is negative AND the probe is negative → positive product → ALSO evidence for this frequency (they agree in being negative together)
When one is positive and the other is negative → negative product → evidence AGAINST
If the signal happens to oscillate at the same frequency as the probe, their peaks and troughs will line up. Every multiplication gives a positive result. The sum is large. The shadow is long.
If the frequencies don't match, sometimes they agree and sometimes they disagree. The positives and negatives cancel out. The sum is near zero. No shadow.
Detecting a frequency in a signal is the same operation as measuring similarity between vectors. Both are projections. Both compute shadows.

props.title: The Phase Problem: Why You Need Complex Numbers

children.children: There's a wrinkle. Suppose your signal contains a sine wave at frequency f, but it's SHIFTED — it starts at a peak instead of at zero. The dot product with sin(2πft) might come out small, not because the frequency is absent, but because the peaks don't line up.
The frequency is present — it's just shifted by 90°. The sine probe misses it because the peaks don't align.
Solution: Probe With BOTH Sine and Cosine
To catch any phase shift, probe with both:

Probe cos: [1, 0.71, 0, -0.71, -1, -0.71, 0, 0.71]  ← cos(t)
Probe sin: [0, 0.71, 1,  0.71,  0, -0.71, -1, -0.71] ← sin(t)

Signal · cos_probe = (1)(1) + (0.71)(0.71) + ... = 4.0  ← BIG!
Signal · sin_probe = (1)(0) + (0.71)(0.71) + ... = 0.0  ← zero

The cosine probe catches it! The frequency was there all along,
just shifted to align with cosine instead of sine.
The two dot products give you two numbers: the 'cosine component' and the 'sine component'. Together, they capture BOTH the strength AND the phase of the frequency.
Enter Complex Numbers: Two Shadows Become One
Instead of tracking two separate numbers, combine them into one complex number:

F(f) = (cosine component) + i × (sine component)
     = 4.0 + i × 0.0
     = 4.0

Magnitude: |F(f)| = √(4² + 0²) = 4    ← HOW MUCH frequency
Phase:     ∠F(f) = arctan(0/4) = 0°    ← WHERE the peaks are
This is Euler's formula in action. The complex number e^(iθ) = cos(θ) + i·sin(θ) encodes BOTH probes simultaneously. The Fourier transform:
F(f) = Σ signal(t) · e^(-i·2πft)

is a single complex dot product that captures magnitude AND phase in one operation. It's not two separate probes — it's one probe that ROTATES in the complex plane, sweeping out both sine and cosine as it spins.

This is why complex numbers appear in physics and signal processing everywhere. They're not an abstract curiosity — they're the natural language for 'shadow plus rotation angle.' Real numbers measure shadow length. Complex numbers measure shadow length AND direction.
Now we can see the whole picture. Every operation below is the same thing: project one object onto another and measure the shadow.

rows: How similar are a and b?
How much of freq f is present?
How much should I attend to this?
Does this operator produce this result?
Angle of the rotation = the operator

children.children: Every row is: multiply corresponding elements, sum the products, interpret the result as 'how much of A lies along B.' The probe changes (sine wave, document vector, attention key), the interpretation changes, but the operation is identical.
The Subterranean Solution
So what IS the deep principle connecting all of these?

An inner product is projection onto a probe. It answers: 'how much of THIS is THAT?' The probe can be a vector, a frequency, a pattern, a question, or a transformation — but the operation is always the same. Multiply pointwise. Sum. Read the shadow.

And when you add complex numbers (Euler's formula), the shadow gains a direction — a phase angle. This lets you measure not just 'how much' but 'in what orientation.' Which is why complex rotations appear in Fourier transforms AND in our Schankian operators — they're both asking 'how much, and at what angle?'
For Machine Learning

Every neural network layer computes dot products (weights · inputs). Every attention mechanism computes dot products (query · key). Every embedding lookup followed by cosine similarity is a dot product. The entire field is built on shadows.
For Signal Processing

The Fourier transform decomposes signals into frequencies by computing the shadow of the signal onto each frequency. The wavelet transform does the same with localized wave packets instead of infinite sine waves. Both are inner products with different probes.
For Our Operators

Schankian operators are complex rotations: e^(iθ). When we score a triple (agent, operator, patient) using ||agent ⊙ operator - patient||, we're asking: 'If I rotate the agent by this operator, does it land on the patient?' The rotation IS the operator. The distance measures how good the shadow is.

And rotations PRESERVE inner products — that's what makes them rotations rather than arbitrary transformations. If two entities were similar before the operator, they're still similar after. The operator changes meaning while preserving the geometric structure of similarity. Just like transposing a melody changes the notes but preserves the intervals.
For Understanding Itself

Perhaps the deepest implication: all measurement is comparison. You can only understand something by holding it up against something you already know and measuring the shadow. A Fourier transform understands a signal by comparing it to pure tones. A neural network understands an input by comparing it to learned patterns. A human understands a new concept by analogy to familiar ones.
Projection is how anything measures anything. The shadow is universal.
Cosine = adjacent / hypotenuse = 'what fraction of this thing lies along that thing.' It ranges from 1 (identical) to 0 (unrelated) to -1 (opposite).
The dot product computes this by multiplying corresponding elements and summing. Multiplication rewards agreement (++ or --) and punishes disagreement (+-).
The Fourier transform is a dot product between a signal and sine/cosine waves. It measures how much of each frequency is 'in' the signal — the shadow of the signal onto each frequency.
Complex numbers (e^(iθ) = cos + i·sin) combine the two probes into one, capturing both magnitude (how much) and phase (at what angle).
Cosine similarity, attention, convolution, and operator scoring are ALL the same operation: project onto a probe, measure the shadow.
The 'subterranean solution' is the inner product — the universal operation for asking 'how much of this is that?'

This tutorial is part of the Schankian Operators research program. The connection to operators: they're complex rotations (e^(iθ)) applied via the same inner product machinery. See the main paper draft for the full technical context.

props.title: Q: Please make a sidebar for this! It's beautiful, but now I want to know where the pieces all come from -- why arctan? why 0/4? -- and what they look like together (visuals). What is it like when a probe rotates in the complex plane??

children.children: Picture the complex plane as a coordinate system where the horizontal axis is "real" (cosine values) and the vertical axis is "imaginary" (sine values). When we write e^(i·2πft), we're describing a point that spins around the origin in a circle as time t advances.

At t=0, our spinning probe points to (1, 0) — pure cosine. As time progresses, it rotates counterclockwise: at 1/4 cycle it's at (0, 1) — pure sine; at 1/2 cycle it's at (-1, 0) — negative cosine; at 3/4 cycle it's at (0, -1) — negative sine. This single rotating point traces out BOTH the cosine wave (its horizontal shadow) and the sine wave (its vertical shadow) simultaneously.

Now here's the magic: when your signal is that shifted cosine wave from the passage, the dot product with our rotating probe gives us a complex number. The magnitude of this number tells us how strong that frequency is, while the angle (computed with arctan) tells us the phase shift. If your cosine wave starts at a peak, the probe's rotation will be perfectly aligned with it at certain angles, giving a large response tilted toward the real axis. The 0/4 notation you saw likely refers to the phase being 0 degrees out of 4 possible quarter-turn positions.

Instead of running two separate probes (sine and cosine) and then combining their results with arctan, the complex exponential probe does it all in one elegant operation. It's like having a detector that not only measures "how much of this frequency is present" but also "when does it peak" — both answers emerge naturally from the single complex result.
Asked about "This is Euler's formula in act..." — 2/14/2026, 10:37:01 AM
